Join the City of Chattanooga Team Chattanooga , a vibrant and growing community of over 190,000 residents within a metropolitan area of nearly 600,000 is known as the "Scenic City" for a reason. Nestled along the Tennessee River and surrounded by the foothills of the Appalachian Mountains, we are a hub of outdoor adventure and innovation. Home to the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) and Chattanooga State Community College , our city blends a rich industrial history with a tech-forward future—famously powered by the nation’s first community-wide "Gig" fiber-optic network.​ The City of Chattanooga was also recently designated as a "National Park City,” the first of its kind in the United States. Our Organization As a City, we are powered by a dedicated workforce of approximately ​ 2,600 employees committed to excellence in public service and making Chattanooga the best community in the country. Our team ensures our city thrives through a wide range of municipal services, including: Public Safety: Chattanooga Police and Fire Departments. Infrastructure Utilities: Public Works, Waste Resources, and specialized Stormwater management. Quality of Life: Award-winning Parks and Outdoors and Community Development systems and the Chattanooga Public Library. Transportation: The Chattanooga Metropolitan Airport (CHA) and citywide transit and pedestrian friendly initiatives. Innovation: Leading-edge smart-city programs with a commitment to cutting edge workforce development policies to ensure all our residents can get ahead. Why Choose Chattanooga? The City of Chattanooga’s workforce is the heartbeat of our community. We don't just offer jobs; we offer careers with purpose: making Chattanooga the best city in America. We are committed to being the premier employer of choice in Southeast Tennessee by providing: Competitive Compensation: Robust pay scales that reflect our commitment to our staff. Work-Life Balance: Generous paid time off and flexible scheduling where possible. Exceptional Wellness: Comprehensive health benefits and wellness programs designed for you and your family that deliver savings to you beyond your paycheck, featuring a centralized health clinic and pharmacy for all City employees. . Growth: A focus on professional development and upward mobility within the organization. ​The Great Outdoors: Your Backyard Playground: Chattanooga isn't just near nature, it's immersed in it. As one of the most biodiverse cities in North America, the "Scenic City" offers an ecological richness that few places on earth can claim. Whether you are an adrenaline junkie or a weekend wanderer, world-class adventure is always less than an hour away. ​Vertical Adventures: Scale legendary sandstone cliffs in what is widely considered the premier rock climbing hub of the Southeast. Water Rapids: From the high-octane whitewater kayaking of the Ocoee River to world-class bass fishing and peaceful paddling on the Tennessee River. Trails for Miles: Explore a massive network of mountain biking and hiking trails that wind through lush ridges, hidden waterfalls, and deep river gorges. In Chattanooga, "work-life balance" means you can finish your shift and be at a trailhead or on the water before the sun sets. Our mission is to work together to build a safe, vibrant, and sustainable city for all Chattanoogans. To do that, it takes a team of skilled and passionate individuals. So, if you’d like to call Chattanooga home – or maybe you already do! – and want to further your career through meaningful work that makes a true impact where you live, we’d love to talk to you. Salary: $16.39 - $19.80 Hourly (GS.03N) Work Hours: Monday – Friday, 7:00 A.M. – 3:30 P.M. (EST) *Hours may vary due to work assignments, or projects.* Work Location: Paul Clark Building, 900 East 11th Street FLSA Status: This is a Non-Exempt position. Department: Public Works - CWS SSA Street Maintenance CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: Incumbents in this classification are responsible for performing miscellaneous labor in the Department of Public Works. Work is performed under close supervision (although most tasks are so routine that little supervision is required). SERIES LEVEL: The City Laborer is a stand-alone classification in the Department of Public Works. ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS: ( The following duties ARE NOT intended to serve as a comprehensive list of all duties performed by all employees in this classification, only a representative summary of the primary duties and responsibilities. Incumbent(s) may not be required to perform all duties listed and may be required to perform additional, position-specific duties.) Performs assorted public works duties such as sweeping sidewalks and streets, blowing and raking leaves and collecting/picking up brush, garbage and recycling materials. May participate in seasonal work such as ice and snow removal. May perform various duties such as operating leaf blowers to remove leaves from various structures/landscapes, sweeping sidewalks and entryways. May perform various duties in stowing scrap metal, delivering and retrieving vehicles from privately operated shops and picking up parts, supplies and mail. May perform various duties in the cleaning and maintenance of various City facilities, buildings and landscapes including cleaning restrooms, sweeping and mopping of flooring, vacuuming, carpet cleaning, operating specialized cleaning equipment, washing windows, removing trash and litter, collecting recycle. May operate various hand and power tools requiring no special training or skill and minor equipment maintenance. May be required to use, carry and answer their cell phone as determined by their job duties and the department head. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Must accomplish the essential functions of the job, with or without reasonable accommodations, in a timely manner. Performs other duties as assigned. This position is deemed essential during inclement weather situations, and must report to or remain at work, even when administrative closings are announced, as determined by the Department Head. DEPARTMENT SPECIFIC DUTIES (if any): When working in Facilities: May set up and breakdown tables and chairs for scheduled meetings. May maintain dumpster enclosures. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: Work requires the ability to read and understand written instructions, to understand simple processes, and the routine operation of power tools and/or small maintenance equipment/machines. High School Diploma or GED preferred, but not required or any combination of equivalent experience and education. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: Valid driver's license may be required depending on assignment. KNOWLEDGE AND SKILLS: Knowledge of construction, grounds or buildings maintenance principles and practices; applicable equipment, materials and tools used in construction, grounds or building maintenance activities and safe work practices. Skill in following oral and written instructions; performing routine maintenance and construction tasks; operating and maintaining applicable construction, grounds and building maintenance equipment and tools and communication and interpersonal skills as applied to interaction with coworkers, supervisor, the general public, etc. sufficient to exchange or convey information and to receive work direction. PHYSICAL DEMANDS: Positions in this class typically require climbing, balancing, stooping, kneeling, crouching, crawling, reaching, standing, walking, pushing, pulling, lifting, fingering, grasping, feeling, talking, hearing, seeing and repetitive motions. WORK ENVIRONMENT VARIES DEPENDENT ON ASSIGNMENT: Medium Work: Exerting up to 50 pounds of force occasionally and/or up to 20 pounds of force frequently and/or up to 10 pounds of force constantly to move objects. Incumbents may be subjected to moving mechanical parts, electrical currents, vibrations, fumes, odors, dusts, gasses, poor ventilation, chemicals, oils, extreme temperatures, inadequate lighting, work space restrictions, intense noises and travel. SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS: Safety Sensitive: Y Department of Transportation - CDL: N Child Sensitive: N SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION: This position requires pre-employment screening(s) that include: background check, drug screening and lift test. The City of Chattanooga, Tennessee is an Equal Opportunity Employer. In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, the City will provide reasonable accommodations to qualified individuals with disabilities and encourage both prospective and current employees to discuss potential accommodations with the employer. 260482 - KS

Department: Public Works - CWS SSA Street Maintenance CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: Incumbents in this classification operate vehicles, tracked or rubber-tired construction and maintenance equipment or other equipment with specialized functions related to maintenance and construction in the Department of Public Works where the operation of complex mechanisms requires a higher degree of skill, dexterity, and finesse, and where work is performed to exacting tolerances. Equipment in this classification includes backhoes, excavators, dozers, graders, and larger off-road vehicles with mechanisms that are complex to operate. Classification is reserved for those positions where the operation off-road, heavy equipment is the primary function of the job. Commercial vehicle operations are also required for some operations. ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS: ( The following duties ARE NOT intended to serve as a comprehensive list of all duties performed by all employees in this classification, only a representative summary of the primary duties and responsibilities. Incumbent(s) may not be required to perform all duties listed and may be required to perform additional, position-specific duties.) Maneuvers heavy equipment and operates complex mechanisms involving digging, moving earth and materials, grading, loading, paving, and performing similar tasks in order to advance projects relating to construction and maintenance of city streets, utilities, and other infrastructure. Equipment is primarily driven and operated on-site, but does require some driving over public roads; maneuvers in and around traffic, crew workers, equipment, utilities, and other obstacles; obeys all traffic laws and safety guidelines; monitors the immediate environment and work area to ensure the safety of the operator, other workers, the equipment and the public. Operates various heavy equipment to assist in the maintenance and upkeep of the landfill; operates bulldozer, compactor, and/or scraper to move and compact waste and other materials to ensure proper placement, level, and contents of landfill mounds; operates same equipment to move and compact earth to provide a proper covering for landfill; keeps metal pile clean and assists with sorting of waste. Operates milling and asphalt paving machines to grind existing pavement and lay down the new pavement for construction and maintenance of roads, parking lots and other paved surfaces; controls speed, depth and width spread/grinding of equipment; operates multiple mechanisms to control speed, volume and depth of asphalt application; monitors and oversees activities of crew workers assisting with the operation of equipment and application of asphalt. Performs basic and semi-skilled labor as appropriate to assist in the completion of maintenance, upkeep and construction projects; picks up paper, limbs and other debris; uses hand tools and operates motorized tools as necessary to complete the assigned task. May be required to use, carry and answer cell phones, use other electronic devices to receive and track work, and/or two-way radio as determined by their job duties and supervisor. Performs other related duties as required. This position is deemed essential during inclement weather situations, and must report to or remain at work, even when administrative closings are announced, as determined by the Department Head. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Must accomplish the essential functions of the job, with or without reasonable accommodations, in a timely manner. May be assigned or reassigned to any division within the department. Performs other duties as assigned. DEPARTMENT SPECIFIC DUTIES (if any): N/A. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: High School Diploma or GED and three (3) years of any combination of relevant education, experience, and/or training sufficient to successfully perform the essential functions of this job or any equivalent combination of education, training, and experience. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: Valid Class D driver’s license KNOWLEDGE AND SKILLS: Knowledge of occupational hazards and safety precautions; general equipment and maintenance principles; materials and equipment utilized in assigned areas of responsibility; demonstrated proficiency on a minimum of three (3) types of heavy equipment to include three (3) years of experience operating heavy equipment. Typical equipment operated might include but is not limited to: excavators, rubber-tired loaders, compactor, scraper, wood chipper, paving machine, milling machine, motor grader, bulldozer, articulated dump truck, and backhoes. MULTIPLE RESCUES MADE BY CFD: Frantic calls to 911 prompted a two-alarm response at a Chattanooga apartment complex late Tuesday night.  At 11:29 PM on 6/23/26, Blue Shift companies were dispatched to 404 Tunnel Boulevard after residents reported being trapped in their apartments by heavy smoke. Several of them fled to their balconies to escape the smoke. One family was trapped on their third floor balcony with small children. Due to entrapment, a second alarm was called to bring additional manpower and resources to the scene. Firefighters quickly started making rescues using ladders to get residents down off their balconies while other crews established a water supply, began making interior searches of apartments to look for other residents, and initiated fire attack. Firefighters made an aggressive push to get the fire under control by 12:03 AM. The cause is under investigation. In total, nine people were rescued from Building E.  Several apartments sustained damage. As a result of the hard work of firefighters and their well-coordinated response, there were no injuries.  Engine 15, Ladder 5, Engine 5, Squad 13, Engine 4, Engine 6, Ladder 6, Squad 1, Engine 1, Engine 10, Engine 8, Ladder 21, Engine 21, Squad 7, Battalion 1, Battalion 2, Battalion 3, and CFD Investigations responded, along with CPD, HCEMS and EPB.  Thanks to our Tri-State Mutual Aid partners for filling in and providing coverage at our fire stations while CFD personnel worked this fire. And thanks to our dispatchers for getting and relaying info needed to save lives.

Last year, we asked Chattanoogans to invest in the essential services that impact their lives: police, fire, paving, affordable housing, and technology to address traffic congestion. This year's budget is about providing a return on that investment.  The approved balanced budget hold's our property tax steady in the face of inflation without cutting services due to a citywide initiative to identify cost savings and hold the line on expenses. The tax rate remains lower than the rate residents pay in Knoxville, Nashville and Memphis. Chattanooga’s tax burden per resident is also lower than Nashville and Knoxville. Swipe to see major investments within the FY27 budget, which begins on July 1.

Today’s Family Justice Center Strategic Summit reminded me once again how powerful a community can be when we come together with a shared purpose. We left the summit with meaningful progress and a stronger vision for promoting safety, healing, and justice for victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, child abuse, elder abuse, and human trafficking. Together, we explored ways to strengthen our coordinated response for adults and children, improve law enforcement partnerships, enhance access to Orders of Protection, engage faith communities, gather meaningful baseline data, expand healthcare and mental health services, strengthen governance, and increase community outreach and engagement. We built on the work from our planning session with Casey Gwinn in March. Casey motivated us and shined the light on best practices l.  Most importantly, every conversation centered on listening to and elevating the voices of survivors. Their experiences, courage, and insights must continue to guide our work. I am incredibly grateful for the many community leaders, professionals, advocates, and partners who invested their time and expertise today. A special thank you to Judge Christie Sell and Councilwoman Jenny Berz for their leadership and commitment to building a safer community for all. The solutions to violence require all of us. If you are interested in being part of this community effort and helping create a coordinated response that supports survivors and their families, we invite you to reach out to the Family Justice Center. Together, we can make a difference. #FamilyJusticeCenter #CommunityCollaboration #SurvivorCentered #EndDomesticViolence #EndSexualAssault #HopeAndHealing #Chattanooga #StrongerTogether

content_text: Join the City of Chattanooga Team Chattanooga , a vibrant and growing community of over 190,000 residents within a metropolitan area of nearly 600,000 is known as the "Scenic City" for a reason. Nestled along the Tennessee River and surrounded by the foothills of the Appalachian Mountains, we are a hub of outdoor adventure and innovation. Home to the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) and Chattanooga State Community College , our city blends a rich industrial history with a tech-forward future—famously powered by the nation’s first community-wide "Gig" fiber-optic network.​ The City of Chattanooga was also recently designated as a "National Park City,” the first of its kind in the United States. Our Organization As a City, we are powered by a dedicated workforce of approximately ​ 2,600 employees committed to excellence in public service and making Chattanooga the best community in the country. Our team ensures our city thrives through a wide range of municipal services, including: Public Safety: Chattanooga Police and Fire Departments. Infrastructure Utilities: Public Works, Waste Resources, and specialized Stormwater management. Quality of Life: Award-winning Parks and Outdoors and Community Development systems and the Chattanooga Public Library. Transportation: The Chattanooga Metropolitan Airport (CHA) and citywide transit and pedestrian friendly initiatives. Innovation: Leading-edge smart-city programs with a commitment to cutting edge workforce development policies to ensure all our residents can get ahead. Why Choose Chattanooga? The City of Chattanooga’s workforce is the heartbeat of our community. We don't just offer jobs; we offer careers with purpose: making Chattanooga the best city in America. We are committed to being the premier employer of choice in Southeast Tennessee by providing: Competitive Compensation: Robust pay scales that reflect our commitment to our staff. Work-Life Balance: Generous paid time off and flexible scheduling where possible. Exceptional Wellness: Comprehensive health benefits and wellness programs designed for you and your family that deliver savings to you beyond your paycheck, featuring a centralized health clinic and pharmacy for all City employees. . Growth: A focus on professional development and upward mobility within the organization. ​The Great Outdoors: Your Backyard Playground: Chattanooga isn't just near nature, it's immersed in it. As one of the most biodiverse cities in North America, the "Scenic City" offers an ecological richness that few places on earth can claim. Whether you are an adrenaline junkie or a weekend wanderer, world-class adventure is always less than an hour away. ​Vertical Adventures: Scale legendary sandstone cliffs in what is widely considered the premier rock climbing hub of the Southeast. Water Rapids: From the high-octane whitewater kayaking of the Ocoee River to world-class bass fishing and peaceful paddling on the Tennessee River. Trails for Miles: Explore a massive network of mountain biking and hiking trails that wind through lush ridges, hidden waterfalls, and deep river gorges. In Chattanooga, "work-life balance" means you can finish your shift and be at a trailhead or on the water before the sun sets. Our mission is to work together to build a safe, vibrant, and sustainable city for all Chattanoogans. To do that, it takes a team of skilled and passionate individuals. So, if you’d like to call Chattanooga home – or maybe you already do! – and want to further your career through meaningful work that makes a true impact where you live, we’d love to talk to you. Salary: $22.74- $27.47 per hour (GS.8) Work Hours: Monday - Friday, 7:00 A.M. - 3:30 P.M. (EST) *Hours may vary due to work assignments and projects.* Work Location: 4063 N Hawthorne St, Chattanooga, TN FLSA Status: This is a Non-Exempt position. Department: Public Works- Solid Waste CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: Incumbents in this classification drive and operate commercial vehicles, tractors, and other equipment with specialized functions that involve the operation of mechanisms to complete tasks in addition to driving and maneuvering the vehicle in order to complete tasks for various solid waste collection, maintenance, forestry and construction projects. Equipment in this classification includes commercial larger vehicles requiring a higher level of skill to drive and maneuver through small residential roads and vehicles with mechanisms that are somewhat complex to operate, requiring more dexterity and finesse. Classification is reserved for those positions where the driving and operating of included equipment is the primary function of the job. SERIES LEVEL: Truck Driver is a stand-alone position. ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS: ( The following duties ARE NOT intended to serve as a comprehensive list of all duties performed by all employees in this classification, only a representative summary of the primary duties and responsibilities. Incumbent(s) may not be required to perform all duties listed and may be required to perform additional, position-specific duties.) Operates various equipment and vehicles to complete tasks related to the maintenance, upkeep, installation and construction of city grounds, property, utilities, rights-of-way, and other infrastructure for Public Works; drives vehicles over public roads, worksites, right-of-way, parks, public works facilities, and other city property; maneuvers in and around traffic, parked cars, equipment, utilities, and other obstacles; obeys all traffic laws and safety guidelines, monitors immediate environment and work are to ensure the safety of the operator, other workers, the equipment and the public. Some positions may drive tandem and tri-axle dump trucks to transport and haul materials between original and final destinations for use in maintenance and construction projects; positions vehicle to allow for the most efficient and effective loading or dumping of materials; secures all doors, gates, and fasteners to prevent spillage or loss of load; operates dumping mechanism to dump materials at appropriate locations. Operates mowing tractors with articulated boom mowing system to mow rights-of-way and other City property along steep slopes, ditches, and hard-to-reach areas inaccessible to most mowing apparatuses; scouts path for and removes rocks, debris and other obstacles that may cause damage to the equipment, property or the public; determines proper settings and adjusts boom for the proper angle, height, and trajectory; maneuvers and monitors boom mower for optimal operation. Operate knuckle-boom truck to collect trash, limbs and other debris from work sites, parks, and other City property and transport it to the landfill; positions vehicle alongside targeted debris; operates truck- or trailer-mounted knuckle-boom arm to pick up debris and load it into the truck; secures all doors, gates, and fasteners to prevent spillage or loss of load during transport; dumps material at an appropriate location. Operates and maneuvers knuckle-boom and articulated arm to pick up limbs; maintains a constant awareness of the environment to ensure the safety of the operator, other crew workers, and the public. Operates street sweepers along a prescribed route to remove dirt and debris from City streets and parking lots; maneuvers in and out of traffic and around parked cars, taking care to reduce congestion and maintain safety; determines proper settings and adjusts brushes for proper angle and height, maintaining proper curb distance for optimum coverage. Drives low-boy and other tractor-trailer rigs to transport heavy construction equipment and materials to and from work-sites; maneuvers equipment to load and unload from the trailer, operating mechanisms as necessary to position for safe transportation. Drives a variety of garbage trucks along a prescribed commercial or residential route to collect refuse; makes frequent stops at appropriate intervals to position truck near refuse containers; retrieves refuse container and attaches to automated dumping mechanism; dumps refuse and returns container; maintains the pace of collection activities to ensure timely completion of the assigned route. Drives fuel truck to transport fuel to equipment at work sites; operates the mechanism to fuel equipment as necessary; applies knowledge of hazardous materials when transporting, handling, and responding to spills involving gasoline, diesel fuel, and other flammable/volatile liquids. Drives bucket and/or lift truck to work site and operates lift mechanisms to elevate crews performing various maintenance, upkeep and installation for traffic systems, tree trimming and other tasks requiring access to high places; positions vehicle and lift-platform to ensure optimum access to the required location and avoid power lines, low branches, traffic and other hazards; monitors activities to ensure the safety of crew workers. Operates a jet-flushing/vacuum/viewer truck for the purpose of cleaning out storm drain and sanitary sewer pipe, manholes, catch basins and other brick and concrete drainage structures in order to maintain the flow throughout the stormwater drainage and sewer waste systems. Drives trucks with a side-mounted articulated vacuum boom along an assigned route for the purpose of picking up leaves, litter, and other yard waste from rights-of-way and out of drainage measurements in order to maintain stormwater drainage flow throughout the entire system. Inspects and prepares equipment for safe and proper operation prior to beginning work for the day; performs light maintenance on equipment which includes refueling and refilling lubricants; cleans vehicles, beds, and mounted equipment upon completion of assignments; secures vehicle after use. Sanitize and clean assigned equipment daily. Performs basic and semi-skilled labor as appropriate to assist in the completion of maintenance, upkeep and construction projects; picks up paper, limbs and other debris; uses hand tools and operates motorized tools as necessary to complete the assigned task. May remove dead animals and trees from the right of way as well as public roads. May answer 911 emergency calls for oil spills as well as black ice. May assist in contacting departments or personnel for an emergency situation. May be required to use, carry and answer cell phones, other devices, and/or two-way radio as determined by their job duties and their supervisor. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Must accomplish the essential functions of the job, with or without reasonable accommodations, in a timely manner. May be assigned or reassigned to any division within the department. Performs other related duties as required. This position is deemed essential during inclement weather situations, and must report to or remain at work, even when administrative closings are announced, as determined by the Department Head. DEPARTMENT SPECIFIC DUTIES (if any): When working in Forestry division: Uses of chainsaws, rigging lines, pulleys, and cranes. Working in high traffic areas with the use of signs, cones, and flaggers and in the vicinity of power lines, moving traffic or directly above buildings. Removes and cleans up dead or hazardous trees. Available on call for storm work or downed trees. May train personnel. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: High School Diploma or GED and one (1) year of any combination of relevant education, experience, and/or training sufficient to successfully perform the essential functions of this job; or any combination of equivalent experience and education. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: Valid Class A, Class A restricted or Class B commercial driver license. Requires appropriate license endorsement(s) related to the area of assignment. KNOWLEDGE SKILLS: Knowledge of occupational hazards and safety precautions; general equipment and maintenance principles; materials and equipment utilized in assigned area of responsibility; demonstrated ability to drive and operate assigned equipment; operation of commercial vehicles that consist of self-contained specialized equipment. Typical equipment operated might include, but is not limited to: tractor-trailers, low boys, fuel trucks, bucket trucks, mechanical street sweepers, knuckle-boom trucks, articulated boom mowers, oil distributors, jet-flushing/vacuum trucks, CCTV equipped trucks and automated garbage trucks with an articulated arm. Must be able to use electronic devices to receive and track work. Position requires minimal, but daily public interaction and must be customer service centered, focused, and able to recognize or resolve customer needs according to departmental practices. PHYSICAL DEMANDS: Positions in this class typically require climbing, balancing, stooping, kneeling, crouching, crawling, reaching, standing, walking, pushing, pulling, lifting, fingering, grasping, feeling, talking, hearing, seeing and repetitive motions. WORK ENVIRONMENT: Medium Work: Exerting up-to 50 pounds of force occasionally, and/or up-to 20 pounds of force frequently, and/or up-to 10 pounds of force constantly to move objects. SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS: Safety Sensitive: Y Department of Transportation - CDL: Y Child Sensitive: N SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION: This position requires pre-employment screening(s) that include: background check / drug screening / lift test The City of Chattanooga, Tennessee is an Equal Opportunity Employer. In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, the City will provide reasonable accommodations to qualified individuals with disabilities and encourage both prospective and current employees to discuss potential accommodations with the employer. How big are the new wastewater storage tanks? Come with us as we take an exclusive look inside…  Checking back in on the e2i2 Equalization Station off of I-75, the equalization tanks are almost ready for use. The West Chickamauga EQ Site features two 15 million gallon tanks for storing excess flow in the sanitary sewer system during heavy rainfall events. This additional storage capacity will prevent sanitary sewer overflows (SSOs) into South Chickamauga Creek and the Tennessee River.  Thank you to the Chattanooga Wastewater Department for the hard work toward this public safety initiative!

No, we’re not building an underground water park. 😎 Today we got a look inside the tanks being built along I-75 near our Brown Acres Golf Course  These giant tanks along I-75 are actually part of a major project that will store up to 30 million gallons of stormwater during heavy rains. Think of them as Chattanooga’s version of a giant “pause button” for excess water and help clean.  They may look like something out of a sci-fi movie now, but by 2027 they’ll help protect our water system and keep local waterways cleaner like the South Chickamauga Creek. Now, this project will not be complete till fall of 2027, which means the greenway from Camp Jordan to Brained Levee will remain closed till then.  Also, if anyone asks, yes… we checked. Echoes are incredible in there. 🎤😂l

Chattanooga was blasted by storms Monday night (6/22/26), making things very busy for Red Shift firefighters across the city. Calls quickly poured into 911, like wires down, fires, flooding, and large trees on homes. We have no injuries to report at this time, thankfully. The structural damage caused by downed trees displaced several families. Trees fell in multiple locations, including:  4100 block of Kirkland Avenue 300 block of S Howell Avenue 800 block of Mount Vernon Circle  2000 block of N Chamberlain Avenue 3300 block of Adkins Road  Please do not drive through flooded roadways or drive over downed power lines.

Brainerd Hills, your new pavilion at Benham-Williams Park is officially open!  Huge kudos to everyone who worked to pull this off! This is the first "in-house" pavilion completely designed and constructed by our world-class team. Thank you to District 6 Councilwoman Jenni Berz and the Brainerd Hills Neighborhood Association for rallying the neighborhood for a rainy-day ribbon cutting and cookout in the park! It was a great chance to put this structure to the test, and it passed with flying colors. We are looking forward to opening more great structures like this in parks across our beautiful city.

Let's send another shout out to civil engineer and project manager, Elizabeth Goss, (holding paper) for letting us tag along and visit the equalization tanks near Brown Acres Golf course.  Both tanks are 240 feet in diameter and provide storage capacity of up to 15 million gallons each of excess wastewater during significant rain events.  This structure diverts excess wastewater, not flood water,  from existing buried sewer system pipeline when excess wastewater otherwise overflows into local waterways.   #CleanWaterMatters #waterquality #cleanwater #PublicWorks #engineers #civilengineering #womeninpublicworks #womenengineersrock

Infrastructure Utilities: Public Works, Waste Resources, and specialized Stormwater management. Quality of Life: Award-winning Parks and Outdoors and Community Development systems and the Chattanooga Public Library. Ability to work in a team-based environment. A complete understanding of OSI model. Ability to communicate effectively verbally and in writing. Ability to problem solve and think logically. A self-starter able to work independently or in a team environment. Ability to be a team player who is self-organized, self-disciplined, and eager to constantly improve themselves and team processes for efficiency and progress. A ribbon was cut today… but the real win happens underground. ✂️💧 The new Kanasita Equalization Basin and Pump Station Project can store up to 5 million gallons of excess wastewater during heavy rain events, helping reduce sanitary sewer overflows and protect our neighborhoods, waterways, and community. And that’s not all—this project also replaced aging infrastructure and addressed long-standing odor concerns for nearby residents. Not every superhero wears a cape. Sometimes it’s a 5-million-gallon tank quietly doing its job beneath the surface. 😉 Today, we celebrate another major milestone for Clear Chattanooga and a cleaner, more resilient future for our city. 📸 Drone photo courtesy of Cool New Media. #ClearChattanooga #Chattanooga #Infrastructure #CommunityFirst #WastewaterWorks

We hear your concerns about the odors, and we want to show you exactly what’s happening behind the scenes at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Upgrading an aging wastewater system is a massive, highly complex process. In the video below, you can get a firsthand look at the major infrastructure upgrades we are currently undertaking to protect our waterways and improve our community: 💡Massive Infrastructure: We manage over 1,200 miles of underground piping and 80 pump stations to process 65 million gallons of wastewater from the surrounding region every single day. 💡Preventing Overflows: To protect the Tennessee River during heavy rains, we are utilizing 30 million gallons of new storage capacity. In fact, the clean water we put back into the river is actually cleaner than the river itself! 💡Odor Control: We know the smell is an issue we need to tackle. We currently utilize five different odor control systems to manage the greenhouse gases emissions from daily sewage, as well as the 80 different local industries that discharge into our system.  💡Green Energy Innovation: We recently announced a new initiative to convert wastewater methane directly into electricity. This will eventually take our campus off the electric grid and save up to $300,000 a month in energy costs. These upgrades take time, but our staff is working hard every day to move our city’s systems forward. Want to better understand the science behind it all? Tap the link below or in our bio for a simple breakdown of exactly how our wastewater treatment process works:👇 https://www.local3news.com/…/article_13c310a8-d534-479f-97c…

Exciting things happening in Chatt 🔥🔥We’re here to answer the question you’ve been asking — what are those gigantic structures popping up along I-75? In a way to combat sanitary sewer overflows, our Wastewater Department has been hard at work with its partners to construct e2i2 Equalization Stations. During a storm event, these will: Send wastewater from the sewer lines to the diversion structure, that then funnels to a 48-inch gravity line (that’s about 20-ft deep) toward the pump storage. The pump storage will then send it to one of the two 15 million-gallon tanks (made of steel and shotcrete). This process will be powered by an electrical house with an 800kw generator and there will also be odor control ventilation for the pump station and tanks. Once the storm event is over, our Wastewater team will send the water downstream through the gravity line that connects to the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus where it will get treated before being sent back to the Tennessee River. Construction began in 2025 and is on track for its 2027 completion. So far, the project has also generated $39 million in local investment and supporting jobs in the region.

Murray Hills Drive temporarily closed for sewer repairs Road expected to reopen March 27, weather permitting CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. (March 5, 2026) — Murray Hills Drive, located off Highway 58, is temporarily closed while crews with the City of Chattanooga Wastewater Department complete sewer repairs. The closure is necessary to repair underground sewer infrastructure and help prevent potential service disruptions. Contractors will perform excavations and point repairs to underground sewer lines through March 18. Once underground work is completed, crews will begin asphalt repairs and concrete restoration, which are expected to be finished by March 25. The roadway is expected to reopen March 27, 2026, after the concrete and asphalt have properly cured. Drivers should follow the approved detour route during the closure and allow additional travel time while work is underway. The construction schedule is weather dependent and subject to change. We're back on Facebook and excited to reconnect with our community! 👋 Did you know the Chattanooga Wastewater Department became an independent department in 2022? We are proud to serve you directly. (Fun fact: Our river system is widely considered the most biodiverse in North America—home to more fish species than all of Europe combined! 🐟). What does it actually take to keep Chattanooga flowing? Here is the breakdown: 🚛 COLLECT: We maintain 1,263 miles of sewer infrastructure (that’s the distance from Chattanooga to Boston!). 🧪 TREAT: We treat 230 million gallons per day at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus (enough to fill 350 Olympic pools). ✅ TRUTH: We don't just flush it. We treat every drop before it returns to the river. Save this post for reference! 📌 • Call 311: For sewer backups, leaky manholes, or billing questions. • Call (423) 643-7400: For sewer taps, location services, or new industry permits. Local historian, Sam Hall, has shared digitized images of the Walnut Street Bridge, sparking discussions about the cost of repairs. However, do stakeholders fully comprehend the intricacies of renovating a structure dating back to the late 19th century? Many assume a fresh coat of paint and replacement of old surface planks would suffice. In reality, the bridge requires extensive structural remediation. What does this entail? Essentially, it means that simply installing a new walkway on a deteriorating foundation is not a viable solution. This massive structure necessitates a thorough examination, and upon peeling away layers of repair, additional areas requiring attention are likely to be uncovered.

The final type of scam we're highlighting is law enforcement scams. In these cases, fraudsters will contact you while impersonating an officer, Federal agent, or bondsman. They'll claim you have an unpaid ticket, missed jury duty, have an unresolved warrant, or that you need to pay bond for a relative in jail, and then try to get you to pay them using gift cards, cryptocurrency, or digital payment services (Zelle, Apple Cash, PayPal, etc.). To be more believable, these scammers may even spoof a real law enforcement agency's phone number, tricking your caller ID. Here's what you need to know: 1. No legitimate law enforcement agency will ask you to pay fees, balances, or bonds with these payment methods. 1. No legitimate law enforcement agency will ask you to pay fees, balances, or bonds with these payment methods. 2. If you receive one of these calls, hang up immediately. 2. If you receive one of these calls, hang up immediately. 3. You can call the law enforcement agency they claim to represent to verify if the call was legitimate. Today's Chattanooga Times Free Press includes a column, "Times Opinion: Chattanooga’s proposed budget has the wrong priorities."  While an opinion piece, facts and context matter. The following was sent to the paper and posted here to provide that information for all who wish to read about CPD's role in immigration enforcement...a topic CPD's Chief of Police has repeatedly publicly explained. Clarifying Mischaracterizations of CPD’s Role in Immigration Enforcement Thank you once again, Ben Sessoms, for providing the Chattanooga Police Department an opportunity to speak to our community about how its officers enforce laws regarding immigration and fill in the context that your column continues to omit. The section of Sessoms’ recent opinion column on the city’s budget that includes a section about immigration enforcement is factually misleading and reflects a misunderstanding of how local law enforcement operates. The assertion that CPD is engaged in “carrying out President Donald Trump’s ongoing mass deportation crackdown locally” mischaracterizes both federal and local roles and confuses the distinct systems of law enforcement that are in place in our city, state, and country.  This is especially important given Sessoms’ claim that “Of the over 500 immigrants transferred to federal immigration custody from the Hamilton County Jail in 2025, a majority of the initial arrests were made by the Chattanooga Police Department.” That statement requires context that his column does not provide. Transfers to federal immigration custody from the jail do not indicate that CPD is engaged in immigration enforcement or selective targeting based on immigration status. Rather, these transfers typically occur through standard criminal justice procedures following arrests for violations of state or local law.  It is important for community members, including local journalists, to understand that local police enforce state and local criminal laws, not federal civil immigration law. Immigration enforcement is the responsibility of federal authorities.  While Sessoms is certainly entitled and even paid to express an opinion, even opinion writers have an obligation to present complete and accurate facts to support their arguments. In this case, he could have easily referenced the broader coverage in the very paper that employs him for the additional context. Specifically, Siena Duncan’s reporting on CPD Chief John Chambers’ presentation to City Council earlier this year explaining in detail how the CPD is, in fact, not acting as an immigration enforcement agency but is operating within established law. Duncan’s reporting also notes that total arrests of Hispanic people by CPD declined 7% in 2025, and arrests of Hispanic people specifically for driving without a license declined 11%.  Again, thank you, Ben and Times Free Press, for the opportunity to present facts to your readers and our community. The most important takeaway from this is simple and straightforward: the Chattanooga Police Department does not make the law, officers enforce the law as written and do not target individuals based on where they come from, what they look like, or the language they speak. Elisa Myzal Director, Public Affairs Chattanooga Police Department

The next type of scam we're highlighting is phishing scams.  In these cases, scammers contact you by phone or email claiming to be from your bank or a company you have an account with (like Apple, Microsoft, etc.). Their goal is to get you to share personal or account information. They may try to trick you into giving these details by saying there's a suspicious charge and that they need you to verify your account number.  Unfortunately, these scammers are often successful. According to the 2025 FBI Internet Crime Report, financial losses from internet crimes are rising. In 2023, $http://12.5 billion in losses were reported, while 2025 saw $http://20.877 billion in reported losses.  If you think you've been the victim of a phishing scam, stop all communication immediately. Alert your bank as soon as possible to freeze your account, and file a police report with your local agency.

PSA: Fraud is on the rise!  Your best defense is knowledge, so over the next few weeks we'll highlight the top three types of scams and what to do if you think you've been the victim of one. First up, romance scams. There's nothing lovely about these scammers. They typically contact their targets on social media or dating sites, and can spend months or YEARS building trust before asking for money. Oh but they've sent you photos or videos? Those can easily be http://A.I. Please do not send money to a romantic partner you've never met in person. Think you've fallen for a romance scam? Here's what to do. 1. Stop all communication immediately 2. Alert your bank to report fraud and freeze your accounts 3. File a police report with your local department 4. Report cybercrime to the FBI at https://hubs.la/Q04kjSHD0 (https://hubs.la/Q04kkkkp0) 5. Report scams to the FTC at https://hubs.la/Q04kjPHK0 (https://hubs.la/Q04kjN6s0)

🤩Inspired by public input beginning in 2023 and in partnership with City of Chattanooga - Government and Hamilton County Mayor’s Office, Chattanooga’s riverfront parks will be transformed into a family-friendly, year-round riverfront park corridor that invites residents and visitors to enjoy the Scenic City’s connection to the Tennessee River.  In addition to the incredible $15 million contribution by the State of Tennessee and $5 million by both the City and the County, our philanthropic community has embraced this project. With their support, we have already raised over $13 million toward our $25 million dollar capital campaign goal! We are well on our way to fully funding the project and establishing a maintenance fund for the park well in advance of construction starting in late November.

Ever wonder how our city parks do double duty as "natural air conditioners" and flood protection?  Our Natural Resources Manager, Anna Mathis, was featured on the National Recreation and Park Association Guardians of Greenspaces Podcast to talk about how we are using nature to tackle extreme summer heat. By "daylighting" streams, taking them out of underground concrete pipes and bringing them back to the surface, our team is creating living ecosystems right in the neighborhoods that need them most.  Tune in: https://shorturl.at/lwxIy #GuardiansOfGreenspaces”

Now that the dust has settled on the month of May, we want to take a second to look back at one of the busiest, most high-energy stretches our recreation facilities have ever seen. For four straight weeks, Chattanooga was the capital of Tennessee sports. We watched college softball powerhouses dominate at Frost Stadium, the state's top youth teams leave everything on the dirt at Warner Park, and elite high school athletes battle for state titles at the Champions Club tennis courts. Pulling off non-stop tournaments of this scale is a massive undertaking. We owe a huge thank you to the team at TSEC for their incredible collaboration in securing these bids and aligning all the moving pieces to bring these events to our city. To the athletes who competed, the families who traveled, and our local community that cheered them on, thank you for making May 2026 a month we won't forget anytime soon. Check out these photos from our NCAA Division II tournament, where the Saint Leo Lions were crowned the National Champions. To celebrate this monumental achievement, they took a dive in the Warner Park Pool! We are so honored to host these amazing athletes. We look forward to hosting many more tournaments in the future! So many great events for America 250! Hope to see you at all of them!Three posters. Three celebrations. One unforgettable summer in Chattanooga.  Introducing the official poster series created by local artist Chad Suggs for three of the City of Chattanooga’s America 250 Celebrations. Today we’re excited to reveal the official poster series for Chattanooga’s America 250 celebrations, created by local artist Chad Suggs. Each design highlights the creativity, culture, and community spirit behind these special events honoring America’s 250th anniversary. The America 250 Celebration Series includes: • June 28 — Democracy Speaks @ Renaissance Park A Poetry Pathways experience featuring poetry from 16 poets displayed on signage throughout Renaissance Park, alongside an evening of live poetry performances, spoken word, live music, and community gathering presented by Rhyme N Chatt and the City of Chattanooga Office of Arts, Culture & Creative Economy • July 2 — Pops on the River @ Coolidge Park A 26-year tradition continues as Chattanooga’s beloved summer celebration returns with live music, fireworks, food trucks, a Homegrown Vendor Market, Kids Zone activities, and performances by the Chattanooga Symphony & Opera • July 3 — Hamilton in the Round @ Renaissance Park A free outdoor sing-along screening of the hit musical Hamilton. Bring your lawn chairs and blankets for an evening of music, storytelling, and community under the stars. Special thanks to Chad Suggs for bringing this series to life and helping celebrate this historic milestone through art and design. This Tennessee America 250 Program is supported in part by the Tennessee Commission for the United States Semiquincentennial Commission.

Chattanooga is about to be booming with celebrations for America's 250th anniversary! From special programming put together by our Office of Arts, Culture and Creative Economy to the beloved Pops on the River — there's plenty of ways to honor over two centuries of patriotism and our history. We're even partnering with Hamilton County to send off Independence Day with the largest drone and pyrotechnics show in local history! Stay tuned as we roll our more details for these events.

Note, this is an ADDITIONAL Celebration for America 250 for Saturday night.  POPS on the River will be held Thursday, July 2nd at Coolidge Park, complete with music and firework show. Going to be an amazing week of celebrations!UPDATED AND CORRECTED: Hamilton County's celebration of America's 250th anniversary will include a drone show, two days after the usual fireworks event on the downtown riverfront. Nature isn’t just a place to visit. It’s a place to return to. 💚🌲 If your week has been feeling a little too fast-paced, consider this your official invitation to slow down. Join us for a Morning Mindfulness Hike at the beautiful Wauhatchie Boulders. Inspired by the Japanese tradition of Shinrin-yoku (forest bathing), this guided experience is all about unplugging, awakening your senses, and cultivating a profound sense of peace. This experience is completely beginner-friendly—no prior experience is needed. 🗓️ Friday, June 5  ⏰ 7:30 AM - 9:00 AM  📍 Wauhatchie Boulders  🔗 Spaces are limited! 🥳Silver Tier Celebration 🥳 Today, the Office of Family Empowerment celebrated its Silver Tier of Excellence accomplishment through the National Community Action Partnership’s Pathways to Excellence Program!  Staff, leadership, and community partners joined us to celebrate the monumental milestone. OFE looks forward to continuing its commitment to the 35 Community Action Standards of Excellence and its dedication to continuous improvement to the families we serve!
